content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 22, 2011


Sandra J. Levin
Colantuono & Levin, PC
555 West 5th Street, 31st Floor
Los Angeles, California 90013

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-11-009

Dear Ms. Levin:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of La Habra Heights City
Councilmember Howard Vipperman regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of
the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as
common law conflict of interest.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Vipperman participate in a governmental decision regarding a code
enforcement action when the action concerns property owned by someone with whom he has a
personal and business relationship?

CONCLUSION

Yes, Councilmember Vipperman may participate in a governmental decision so long as
there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of his economic interests, as
discussed below.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-009
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of La Habra Heights, and are writing on behalf of
La Habra Heights City Councilmember Howard Vipperman. This is a follow up to a previous
advice request concerning the same governmental decision before the La Habra Heights City
Council regarding a code enforcement action on a property owned by James and Nancy Luis.

James Luis is Vice President of Shepard Brothers, Inc., which regularly engages in
transactions with Vip Rubber Co., Inc, a company in which Councilmember Vipperman has an
interest. Councilmember Vipperman owns 14 percent of the stock in Vip Rubber Co., Inc and
he is the executor of a trust that owns a further 37 percent of the stock. You have not stated that
he has any interest in this 37 percent. Councilmember Vipperman earns a salary from Vip
Rubber Co., Inc. as well. However, Councilmember Vipperman has not earned profits or
received dividends from Vip Rubber Co. Inc., within the past 12 months.

During the past 12 months Vip Rubber Co., Inc. has purchased goods from Shepard
Brothers, Inc. valued in excess of $2,200 and sold goods to Shepard Brothers, Inc. valued in
excess of $1,300. Vip Rubber Co., Inc.'s profit on the sale of goods is less than 10 percent.
James Luis is the Vice President of Shepard Brothers, Inc., but has no ownership interest in the
company. Councilmember Vipperman also has a personal relationship with James Luis and they
are both members of the same golf club.

ANALYSIS

As discussed in our previous letter, the Commission has adopted an eight step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation
18700(b).) In this case steps one and two are met as Councilmember Vipperman is a public
official who will be called upon to make a governmental decision regarding the code
enforcement action. (Section 82048).

With respect to step three, Councilmember Vipperman has an economic interest in Vip
Rubber Co., Inc. as a source of income if he receives income from Vip Rubber Co. (either
through profit or a salary) of $300 or more. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) Your facts
indicate he has not received any profit from Vip Rubber Co., Inc. in the past 12 months,
though he does receive a salary. Councilmember Vipperman also has an economic interest in
Vip Rubber Co., Inc. if his he has an investment of $2,000. (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)

Additionally, Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also includes a
pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse
owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater. From your facts,
however, it does not appear that Councilmember Vipperman's 14 percent pro rata share in the
income from James Luis or Shepard Brothers, Inc. equals $500 or more in the past 12 months.

File No. A-11-009
Page No. 3


Councilmember Vipperman's only economic interest at issue is his interest in Vip Rubber
Co., Inc., assuming he has received a salary of $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision, or he has an investment interest in Vip Rubber Co., Inc of $2,000 or more. Vip Rubber
Co., Inc., Vip Rubber Co., Inc. is indirectly involved in the governmental decision at hand
because the company has not initiated the code enforcement action and is not a named party in or
the subject of the decision. (Regulation 18704.1).

A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a)). Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the applicable materiality standard for an economic
interest that is indirectly involved and is based on the size of the business. Assuming Vip
Rubber Co., Inc. is a relatively small business with a net income of less than $750,000 of a year,
subdivision (c)(4) sets forth the applicable materiality standard.

"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(1)-(3), the financial
effect of a governmental on the business entity is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more."

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

The next step is to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standards listed above will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality
standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether
the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be
certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the
determination through a good faith effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock Advice
Letter, No, A-01-140; O'Harra Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)

However, from the facts you have provided it is unlikely that there will be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon Councilmember Vipperman's economic interest, and
he would not be disqualified from participating in the governmental decision under the Act.

File No. A-11-009
Page No. 4


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

enforcement action when the action concerns property owned by someone with whom he has a 
personal and business relationship?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Councilmember Vipperman may participate in a governmental decision so long as 
there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of his economic interests, as 
discussed below.All 
regulatory references are to Tille 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
